items.html: Clearly define the concept of allyship in the context of healthcare, specifically within the realm of sickle cell disease
Identify the key characteristics and behaviors of effective allies in the healthcare setting
Recognize the importance of intersectionality in allyship, considering how various factors such as race, gender, and socio-economic status intersect with care of people with sickle cell disease.

slides.title: How Do We Move Towards Anti-Racist Praxis in Healthcare?

images.caption: Please click and zoom into the text. Photo credit: Crear-Perry et. al., 2020

slides.title: How do we Practice Critical Allyship?

data.html: How can we, as healthcare providers, flip the coin?

items.html: Do not use your expertise to help marginalized groups with 'their problems', rather, use your expertise to dismantle the system that made these coins possible
Step back – make space for those on the other side of the coin.
Change is needed at the interpersonal level, but also at the systemic and institutional levels.
Allyship does not equal enhancement of personal power.

slides.title: Do not use your expertise to help _____ groups

data.html: Do not use your expertise to help marginalized groups with 'their problems', rather, use your expertise to dismantle the system that made these coins possible.
Allyship is about having someone's back. It's the ongoing process of advocating, supporting, and being a voice for people whose voices aren't being heard.

images.caption: Allyship is about having someone's back. It's the ongoing process of advocating, supporting, and being a voice for people whose voices aren't being heard.
As an ally, you are not a bystander; you are an upstander.
Allyship is active, and an ally recognizes the privilege that they receive from society's patterns of inequality and injustice and takes responsibility for changing those patterns.
As an ally, you are using an aspect of your privilege to help someone who is being harmed.

slides.title: How to be an Ally: 1. Listen

images.caption: How to be an ally: 1. Listen
We have to start by listening more and speaking less to learn about the social identities of the people and populations that we see
Work with diverse patient groups. Working with social, cultural and/or religious advocates for patient groups of differing social identities.
Engage with students and faculty across social groups. Leaders, educators and preceptors from underrepresented groups.

Embrace and normalize discussions about privilege and complex social issues. We have to be willing to make mistakes and embrace that discomfort during difficult conversations. The truth is that if you are never uncomfortable as an ally, you probably aren't doing it right!

Do not ask underrepresented group members to be your educators. Engage in self-education. We should not look to our under-represented peers and colleagues as educators. There may be some who are passionate about EDI and education, but it is ultimately our responsibility to educate ourselves.
Recognize that our needs are secondary when we are being allies. As allies, we must put the needs of our patients' first.

6. Do not expect reward or recognition. Be an active ally without expectation. As allies, we should recognize that our needs are secondary and that we are not using our privilege to save others but rather to speak up for others when they do not have the opportunity to do that. There are no rewards or recognition that should come with that.

slides.title: How to be an Ally: 2. Reflect

images.caption: Please click to zoom into the image. Photo credit: Tervalon & Murray-García, 1998

data.html:
"A lifelong process of self-reflection and self-critique whereby the individual not only learns about another's culture, but one starts with an examination of her/his own beliefs and cultural identities."

Through our self-reflection process, we should reflect upon our social power in relation to that of our patients, colleagues, learners, etc. Again, we are back to that wheel of power and privilege.

What is your own privilege? And what about those who surround you as friends, colleagues and supports?

We have to understand where we are coming from and what privileges we bring with us so that we can be effective allies.

images.caption: Please click to zoom into the image. Photo credit: https://cihr-irsc.gc.ca/images/igh_wheel-en.jpg

slides.title: How to be an Ally: 3. Grow

data.html:
As we become better at listening and reflecting on social identities and their impacts on us and those around us, we will grow to become more effective allies.

You have probably seen different versions of this learning and growth continuum. I adapted this from a version related to becoming anti-racist.

images.caption: Please click and zoom into the various sections. Photo credit: Adapted from Ibrahim, A., https://www.mcgill.ca/thewelloffice/files/thewelloffice/becomingantiracist.pdf

data.html:
Overcoming our biases to be effective allies is not an easy process. It will begin with personal reflection and will start from a place of fear and discomfort.

By actively seeking to know and understand the presence of bias and its influence on us as clinicians, teachers and learners in medicine, we will move from the stage of fear to learning and ultimately growing in our cultural humility, understanding and allyship.
Swipe the action to the right if it is aligned with the practice of Allyship (DO) and to the left if it is not (DON'T).

data.question: Work with diverse patient groups.
data.feedback_correct: Working with diverse patient groups is aligned with allyship practice — it supports listening to and learning from social, cultural and/or religious advocates for patient groups of differing social identities.

data.question: Engage with students and faculty across a specific social group.
data.feedback_correct: Allyship requires engaging with students and faculty across social groups — not limiting engagement to a specific social group. Leaders, educators and preceptors from underrepresented groups should all be included.

data.question: Embrace and normalize discussions about privilege and complex social issues.
data.feedback_correct: Embracing and normalizing discussions about privilege and complex social issues is a core practice of allyship. We have to be willing to make mistakes and embrace discomfort during difficult conversations.

data.question: Ask underrepresented group members to be your educators.
data.feedback_correct: Do not ask underrepresented group members to be your educators. Engage in self-education. It is ultimately our responsibility to educate ourselves, not to look to our under-represented peers and colleagues as educators.

data.question: Recognize that our needs are the priority when we are being allies.
data.feedback_correct: Recognize that our needs are secondary when we are being allies. As allies, we must put the needs of our patients first.

data.question: Be conscious, aware and considerate.
data.feedback_correct: Allyship requires more than passive consciousness and consideration — it demands active solidarity, dismantling systems of inequality, and recognizing that our needs as allies are secondary to those we are supporting.

data.question: Do not expect reward or recognition.
data.feedback_correct: Be an active ally without expectation. As allies, we should recognize that our needs are secondary and that we are not using our privilege to save others but rather to speak up for others when they do not have the opportunity to do that. There are no rewards or recognition that should come with that.

data.question: What is defined as "a lifelong process of self-reflection and self-critique whereby the individual not only learns about another's culture but starts with an examination of her/his own beliefs and cultural identities"?
data.feedback_correct: Cultural humility is defined as a lifelong process of self-reflection and self-critique, beginning with an examination of one's own beliefs and cultural identities before learning about others' cultures (Tervalon & Murray-García, 1998).

data.html:
Place these zones in order representing the steps in overcoming our biases to be effective allies:

  1. Comfort Zone
  2. Fear Zone
  3. Learning Zone
  4. Growth Zone

In order to overcome our biases to be effective allies, these are the zones you must go through: Comfort Zone → Fear Zone → Learning Zone → Growth Zone

items.html: Use your privilege to dismantle the system that made these coins possible
In order to flip the coin of oppression, there has to be acknowledgement that the coin exists and there are two sides.

Cultural Humility (a.k.a. cultural sensitivity, cultural curiosity) "A lifelong process of self-reflection and self-critique whereby the individual not only learns about another's culture, but one starts with an examination of her/his own beliefs and cultural identities." We have to start with ourselves before we can stand in solidarity with others.
Adapted from Andrew M. Ibrahim MD, MSc, https://www.mcgill.ca/thewelloffice/files/thewelloffice/becomingantiracist.pdf
Crear-Perry, J., Maybank, A., Keeys, M., Mitchell, N., & Godbolt, D. (2020). Moving towards anti-racist praxis in medicine. Lancet, 396(10249):451-453

Tervalon, M., Murray-García, J. (1998). Cultural Humility Versus Cultural Competence: A Critical Distinction in Defining Physician Training Outcomes in Multicultural Education. Journal of Health Care for the Poor and Underserved, 9(2), 117-125
